speaker: CAESAR
text: Calphurnia.

speaker: CASCA
text: Peace, ho! Caesar speaks.

speaker: CAESAR
text: Calphurnia.

speaker: CALPHURNIA
text: Here, my lord.

speaker: CAESAR
text: Stand you directly in Antonius’ way

speaker: CAESAR
text: When he doth run his course.—Antonius.

speaker: ANTONY
text: Caesar, my lord.

speaker: CAESAR
text: Forget not in your speed, Antonius,

speaker: CAESAR
text: To touch Calphurnia, for our elders say

speaker: CAESAR
text: The barren, touchèd in this holy chase,

speaker: CAESAR
text: Shake off their sterile curse.

speaker: ANTONY
text: I shall remember.

speaker: ANTONY
text: When Caesar says “Do this,” it is performed.

speaker: CAESAR
text: Set on and leave no ceremony out.

speaker: SOOTHSAYER
text: Caesar.

speaker: CAESAR
text: Ha! Who calls?

speaker: CASCA
text: Bid every noise be still. Peace, yet again!

speaker: CAESAR
text: Who is it in the press that calls on me?

speaker: CAESAR
text: I hear a tongue shriller than all the music

speaker: CAESAR
text: Cry “Caesar.” Speak. Caesar is turned to hear.

speaker: SOOTHSAYER
text: Beware the ides of March.

speaker: CAESAR
text: What man is that?

speaker: BRUTUS
text: A soothsayer bids you beware the ides of March.

speaker: CAESAR
text: Set him before me. Let me see his face.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Fellow, come from the throng.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Look upon Caesar.

speaker: CAESAR
text: What sayst thou to me now? Speak once again.

speaker: SOOTHSAYER
text: Beware the ides of March.

speaker: CAESAR
text: He is a dreamer. Let us leave him. Pass.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Will you go see the order of the course?

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Not I.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: I pray you, do.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: I am not gamesome. I do lack some part

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Of that quick spirit that is in Antony.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Let me not hinder, Cassius, your desires.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: I’ll leave you.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Brutus, I do observe you now of late.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: I have not from your eyes that gentleness

speaker: CASSIUS
text: And show of love as I was wont to have.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: You bear too stubborn and too strange a hand

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Over your friend that loves you.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Cassius,

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Be not deceived. If I have veiled my look,

speaker: BRUTUS
text: I turn the trouble of my countenance

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Merely upon myself. Vexèd I am

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Of late with passions of some difference,

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Conceptions only proper to myself,

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Which give some soil, perhaps, to my behaviors.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: But let not therefore my good friends be grieved

speaker: BRUTUS
text: (Among which number, Cassius, be you one)

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Nor construe any further my neglect

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Than that poor Brutus, with himself at war,

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Forgets the shows of love to other men.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Then, Brutus, I have much mistook your passion,

speaker: CASSIUS
text: By means whereof this breast of mine hath buried

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Thoughts of great value, worthy cogitations.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Tell me, good Brutus, can you see your face?

speaker: BRUTUS
text: No, Cassius, for the eye sees not itself

speaker: BRUTUS
text: But by reflection, by some other things.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: ’Tis just.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: And it is very much lamented, Brutus,

speaker: CASSIUS
text: That you have no such mirrors as will turn

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Your hidden worthiness into your eye,

speaker: CASSIUS
text: That you might see your shadow. I have heard

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Where many of the best respect in Rome,

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Except immortal Caesar, speaking of Brutus

speaker: CASSIUS
text: And groaning underneath this age’s yoke,

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Have wished that noble Brutus had his eyes.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Into what dangers would you lead me, Cassius,

speaker: BRUTUS
text: That you would have me seek into myself

speaker: BRUTUS
text: For that which is not in me?

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Therefore, good Brutus, be prepared to hear.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: And since you know you cannot see yourself

speaker: CASSIUS
text: So well as by reflection, I, your glass,

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Will modestly discover to yourself

speaker: CASSIUS
text: That of yourself which you yet know not of.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: And be not jealous on me, gentle Brutus.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Were I a common laughter, or did use

speaker: CASSIUS
text: To stale with ordinary oaths my love

speaker: CASSIUS
text: To every new protester; if you know

speaker: CASSIUS
text: That I do fawn on men and hug them hard

speaker: CASSIUS
text: And after scandal them, or if you know

speaker: CASSIUS
text: That I profess myself in banqueting

speaker: CASSIUS
text: To all the rout, then hold me dangerous.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: What means this shouting? I do fear the people

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Choose Caesar for their king.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Ay, do you fear it?

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Then must I think you would not have it so.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: I would not, Cassius, yet I love him well.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: But wherefore do you hold me here so long?

speaker: BRUTUS
text: What is it that you would impart to me?

speaker: BRUTUS
text: If it be aught toward the general good,

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Set honor in one eye and death i’ th’ other

speaker: BRUTUS
text: And I will look on both indifferently;

speaker: BRUTUS
text: For let the gods so speed me as I love

speaker: BRUTUS
text: The name of honor more than I fear death.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: I know that virtue to be in you, Brutus,

speaker: CASSIUS
text: As well as I do know your outward favor.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Well, honor is the subject of my story.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: I cannot tell what you and other men

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Think of this life; but, for my single self,

speaker: CASSIUS
text: I had as lief not be as live to be

speaker: CASSIUS
text: In awe of such a thing as I myself.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: I was born free as Caesar; so were you;

speaker: CASSIUS
text: We both have fed as well, and we can both

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Endure the winter’s cold as well as he.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: For once, upon a raw and gusty day,

speaker: CASSIUS
text: The troubled Tiber chafing with her shores,

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Caesar said to me “Dar’st thou, Cassius, now

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Leap in with me into this angry flood

speaker: CASSIUS
text: And swim to yonder point?” Upon the word,

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Accoutered as I was, I plungèd in

speaker: CASSIUS
text: And bade him follow; so indeed he did.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: The torrent roared, and we did buffet it

speaker: CASSIUS
text: With lusty sinews, throwing it aside

speaker: CASSIUS
text: And stemming it with hearts of controversy.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: But ere we could arrive the point proposed,

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Caesar cried “Help me, Cassius, or I sink!”

speaker: CASSIUS
text: I, as Aeneas, our great ancestor,

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Did from the flames of Troy upon his shoulder

speaker: CASSIUS
text: The old Anchises bear, so from the waves of Tiber

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Did I the tired Caesar. And this man

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Is now become a god, and Cassius is

speaker: CASSIUS
text: A wretched creature and must bend his body

speaker: CASSIUS
text: If Caesar carelessly but nod on him.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: He had a fever when he was in Spain,

speaker: CASSIUS
text: And when the fit was on him, I did mark

speaker: CASSIUS
text: How he did shake. ’Tis true, this god did shake.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: His coward lips did from their color fly,

speaker: CASSIUS
text: And that same eye whose bend doth awe the world

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Did lose his luster. I did hear him groan.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Ay, and that tongue of his that bade the Romans

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Mark him and write his speeches in their books,

speaker: CASSIUS
text: “Alas,” it cried “Give me some drink, Titinius”

speaker: CASSIUS
text: As a sick girl. You gods, it doth amaze me

speaker: CASSIUS
text: A man of such a feeble temper should

speaker: CASSIUS
text: So get the start of the majestic world

speaker: CASSIUS
text: And bear the palm alone.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Another general shout!

speaker: BRUTUS
text: I do believe that these applauses are

speaker: BRUTUS
text: For some new honors that are heaped on Caesar.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Why, man, he doth bestride the narrow world

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Like a Colossus, and we petty men

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Walk under his huge legs and peep about

speaker: CASSIUS
text: To find ourselves dishonorable graves.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Men at some time are masters of their fates.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: The fault, dear Brutus, is not in our stars,

speaker: CASSIUS
text: But in ourselves, that we are underlings.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: “Brutus” and “Caesar”—what should be in that “Caesar”?

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Why should that name be sounded more than yours?

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Write them together, yours is as fair a name;

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Sound them, it doth become the mouth as well;

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Weigh them, it is as heavy; conjure with ’em,

speaker: CASSIUS
text: “Brutus” will start a spirit as soon as “Caesar.”

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Now, in the names of all the gods at once,

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Upon what meat doth this our Caesar feed

speaker: CASSIUS
text: That he is grown so great? Age, thou art shamed!

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Rome, thou hast lost the breed of noble bloods!

speaker: CASSIUS
text: When went there by an age, since the great flood,

speaker: CASSIUS
text: But it was famed with more than with one man?

speaker: CASSIUS
text: When could they say, till now, that talked of Rome,

speaker: CASSIUS
text: That her wide walks encompassed but one man?

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Now is it Rome indeed, and room enough

speaker: CASSIUS
text: When there is in it but one only man.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: O, you and I have heard our fathers say

speaker: CASSIUS
text: There was a Brutus once that would have brooked

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Th’ eternal devil to keep his state in Rome

speaker: CASSIUS
text: As easily as a king.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: That you do love me, I am nothing jealous.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: What you would work me to, I have some aim.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: How I have thought of this, and of these times,

speaker: BRUTUS
text: I shall recount hereafter. For this present,

speaker: BRUTUS
text: I would not, so with love I might entreat you,

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Be any further moved. What you have said

speaker: BRUTUS
text: I will consider; what you have to say

speaker: BRUTUS
text: I will with patience hear, and find a time

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Both meet to hear and answer such high things.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Till then, my noble friend, chew upon this:

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Brutus had rather be a villager

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Than to repute himself a son of Rome

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Under these hard conditions as this time

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Is like to lay upon us.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: I am glad that my weak words

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Have struck but thus much show of fire from Brutus.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: The games are done, and Caesar is returning.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: As they pass by, pluck Casca by the sleeve,

speaker: CASSIUS
text: And he will, after his sour fashion, tell you

speaker: CASSIUS
text: What hath proceeded worthy note today.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: I will do so. But look you, Cassius,

speaker: BRUTUS
text: The angry spot doth glow on Caesar’s brow,

speaker: BRUTUS
text: And all the rest look like a chidden train.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Calphurnia’s cheek is pale, and Cicero

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Looks with such ferret and such fiery eyes

speaker: BRUTUS
text: As we have seen him in the Capitol,

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Being crossed in conference by some senators.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Casca will tell us what the matter is.

speaker: CAESAR
text: Antonius.

speaker: ANTONY
text: Caesar.

speaker: CAESAR
text: Let me have men about me that are fat,

speaker: CAESAR
text: Sleek-headed men, and such as sleep a-nights.

speaker: CAESAR
text: Yond Cassius has a lean and hungry look.

speaker: CAESAR
text: He thinks too much. Such men are dangerous.

speaker: ANTONY
text: Fear him not, Caesar; he’s not dangerous.

speaker: ANTONY
text: He is a noble Roman, and well given.

speaker: CAESAR
text: Would he were fatter! But I fear him not.

speaker: CAESAR
text: Yet if my name were liable to fear,

speaker: CAESAR
text: I do not know the man I should avoid

speaker: CAESAR
text: So soon as that spare Cassius. He reads much,

speaker: CAESAR
text: He is a great observer, and he looks

speaker: CAESAR
text: Quite through the deeds of men. He loves no plays,

speaker: CAESAR
text: As thou dost, Antony; he hears no music;

speaker: CAESAR
text: Seldom he smiles, and smiles in such a sort

speaker: CAESAR
text: As if he mocked himself and scorned his spirit

speaker: CAESAR
text: That could be moved to smile at anything.

speaker: CAESAR
text: Such men as he be never at heart’s ease

speaker: CAESAR
text: Whiles they behold a greater than themselves,

speaker: CAESAR
text: And therefore are they very dangerous.

speaker: CAESAR
text: I rather tell thee what is to be feared

speaker: CAESAR
text: Than what I fear; for always I am Caesar.

speaker: CAESAR
text: Come on my right hand, for this ear is deaf,

speaker: CAESAR
text: And tell me truly what thou think’st of him.

speaker: CASCA
text: You pulled me by the cloak. Would you speak

speaker: CASCA
text: with me?

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Ay, Casca. Tell us what hath chanced today

speaker: BRUTUS
text: That Caesar looks so sad.

speaker: CASCA
text: Why, you were with him, were you not?

speaker: BRUTUS
text: I should not then ask Casca what had chanced.

speaker: CASCA
text: Why, there was a crown offered him; and, being

speaker: CASCA
text: offered him, he put it by with the back of his hand,

speaker: CASCA
text: thus, and then the people fell a-shouting.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: What was the second noise for?

speaker: CASCA
text: Why, for that too.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: They shouted thrice. What was the last cry for?

speaker: CASCA
text: Why, for that too.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Was the crown offered him thrice?

speaker: CASCA
text: Ay, marry, was ’t, and he put it by thrice, every

speaker: CASCA
text: time gentler than other; and at every putting-by,

speaker: CASCA
text: mine honest neighbors shouted.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Who offered him the crown?

speaker: CASCA
text: Why, Antony.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Tell us the manner of it, gentle Casca.

speaker: CASCA
text: I can as well be hanged as tell the manner of it.

speaker: CASCA
text: It was mere foolery; I did not mark it. I saw Mark

speaker: CASCA
text: Antony offer him a crown (yet ’twas not a crown

speaker: CASCA
text: neither; ’twas one of these coronets), and, as I told

speaker: CASCA
text: you, he put it by once; but for all that, to my

speaker: CASCA
text: thinking, he would fain have had it. Then he offered

speaker: CASCA
text: it to him again; then he put it by again; but to my

speaker: CASCA
text: thinking, he was very loath to lay his fingers off it.

speaker: CASCA
text: And then he offered it the third time. He put it the

speaker: CASCA
text: third time by, and still as he refused it the rabblement

speaker: CASCA
text: hooted and clapped their chopped hands and

speaker: CASCA
text: threw up their sweaty nightcaps and uttered such a

speaker: CASCA
text: deal of stinking breath because Caesar refused the

speaker: CASCA
text: crown that it had almost choked Caesar, for he

speaker: CASCA
text: swooned and fell down at it. And for mine own part,

speaker: CASCA
text: I durst not laugh for fear of opening my lips and

speaker: CASCA
text: receiving the bad air.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: But soft, I pray you. What, did Caesar swoon?

speaker: CASCA
text: He fell down in the marketplace and foamed at

speaker: CASCA
text: mouth and was speechless.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: ’Tis very like; he hath the falling sickness.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: No, Caesar hath it not; but you and I

speaker: CASSIUS
text: And honest Casca, we have the falling sickness.

speaker: CASCA
text: I know not what you mean by that, but I am

speaker: CASCA
text: sure Caesar fell down. If the tag-rag people did not

speaker: CASCA
text: clap him and hiss him, according as he pleased and

speaker: CASCA
text: displeased them, as they use to do the players in the

speaker: CASCA
text: theater, I am no true man.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: What said he when he came unto himself?

speaker: CASCA
text: Marry, before he fell down, when he perceived

speaker: CASCA
text: the common herd was glad he refused the crown,

speaker: CASCA
text: he plucked me ope his doublet and offered them his

speaker: CASCA
text: throat to cut. An I had been a man of any occupation,

speaker: CASCA
text: if I would not have taken him at a word, I

speaker: CASCA
text: would I might go to hell among the rogues. And so

speaker: CASCA
text: he fell. When he came to himself again, he said if he

speaker: CASCA
text: had done or said anything amiss, he desired their

speaker: CASCA
text: Worships to think it was his infirmity. Three or four

speaker: CASCA
text: wenches where I stood cried “Alas, good soul!” and

speaker: CASCA
text: forgave him with all their hearts. But there’s no

speaker: CASCA
text: heed to be taken of them; if Caesar had stabbed

speaker: CASCA
text: their mothers, they would have done no less.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: And, after that, he came thus sad away?

speaker: CASCA
text: Ay.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Did Cicero say anything?

speaker: CASCA
text: Ay, he spoke Greek.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: To what effect?

speaker: CASCA
text: Nay, an I tell you that, I’ll ne’er look you i’ th’

speaker: CASCA
text: face again. But those that understood him smiled at

speaker: CASCA
text: one another and shook their heads. But for mine

speaker: CASCA
text: own part, it was Greek to me. I could tell you more

speaker: CASCA
text: news too: Marullus and Flavius, for pulling scarves

speaker: CASCA
text: off Caesar’s images, are put to silence. Fare you

speaker: CASCA
text: well. There was more foolery yet, if I could remember

speaker: CASCA
text: it.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Will you sup with me tonight, Casca?

speaker: CASCA
text: No, I am promised forth.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Will you dine with me tomorrow?

speaker: CASCA
text: Ay, if I be alive, and your mind hold, and your

speaker: CASCA
text: dinner worth the eating.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Good. I will expect you.

speaker: CASCA
text: Do so. Farewell both.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: What a blunt fellow is this grown to be!

speaker: BRUTUS
text: He was quick mettle when he went to school.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: So is he now in execution

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Of any bold or noble enterprise,

speaker: CASSIUS
text: However he puts on this tardy form.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: This rudeness is a sauce to his good wit,

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Which gives men stomach to digest his words

speaker: CASSIUS
text: With better appetite.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: And so it is. For this time I will leave you.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Tomorrow, if you please to speak with me,

speaker: BRUTUS
text: I will come home to you; or, if you will,

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Come home to me, and I will wait for you.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: I will do so. Till then, think of the world.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Well, Brutus, thou art noble. Yet I see

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Thy honorable mettle may be wrought

speaker: CASSIUS
text: From that it is disposed. Therefore it is meet

speaker: CASSIUS
text: That noble minds keep ever with their likes;

speaker: CASSIUS
text: For who so firm that cannot be seduced?

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Caesar doth bear me hard, but he loves Brutus.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: If I were Brutus now, and he were Cassius,

speaker: CASSIUS
text: He should not humor me. I will this night

speaker: CASSIUS
text: In several hands in at his windows throw,

speaker: CASSIUS
text: As if they came from several citizens,

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Writings, all tending to the great opinion

speaker: CASSIUS
text: That Rome holds of his name, wherein obscurely

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Caesar’s ambition shall be glancèd at

speaker: CASSIUS
text: And after this, let Caesar seat him sure,

speaker: CASSIUS
text: For we will shake him, or worse days endure.
